news_summary: NASA's historic Artemis mission successfully launched astronauts toward the Moon, marking a significant milestone for humanity while new research provides evidence on black-hole mass gaps.
news_summary_fr: La mission historique Artemis de la NASA a permis d'envoyer avec succès des astronautes vers la Lune, marquant ainsi une étape importante pour l'humanité, tandis que de nouvelles recherches apportent des preuves concernant les écarts de masse des trous noirs.
news_summary_es: La histórica misión Artemis de la NASA lanzó con éxito a los astronautas hacia la Luna, lo que supone un hito significativo para la humanidad, mientras que nuevas investigaciones aportan pruebas sobre las discrepancias en la masa de los agujeros negros.

articles.title: Liftoff! NASA Launches Astronauts on Historic Artemis Moon Mission
articles.summary: Spurred by American ingenuity, astronauts on NASA’s Artemis II mission are in flight, preparing for the first crewed lunar flyby in more than 50 years. NASA’s SLS (Space Launch System) rocket lifted off from Launch Pad 39B at the agency’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida at 6:35 p.m. EDT Wednesday, sending four astronauts aboard the […]
articles.content:
The SLS (Space Launch System) launches with the Artemis II crew aboard the Orion spacecraft on April 1, 2026, at NASA’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida. Credit: NASA/Bill Ingalls

Spurred by American ingenuity, astronauts on NASA’s Artemis II mission are in flight, preparing for the first crewed lunar flyby in more than 50 years.

NASA’s SLS (Space Launch System) rocket lifted off from Launch Pad 39B at the agency’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida at 6:35 p.m. EDT Wednesday, sending four astronauts aboard the Orion spacecraft on a planned test flight around the Moon and back.

“Today’s launch marks a defining moment for our nation and for all who believe in exploration. Artemis II builds on the vision set by President Donald J. Trump, returning humanity to the Moon for the first time in more than 50 years and opening the next chapter of lunar exploration beyond Apollo. Aboard Orion are four remarkable explorers preparing for the first crewed flight of this rocket and spacecraft, a true test mission that will carry them farther and faster than any humans in a generation,” said NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman. “Artemis II is the start of something bigger than any one mission. It marks our return to the Moon, not just to visit, but to eventually stay on our Moon Base, and lays the foundation for the next giant leaps ahead.”

The successful launch is the beginning of an approximately 10-day mission for NASA astronauts Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and CSA (Canadian Space Agency) astronaut Jeremy Hansen. As the first crewed mission of NASA’s Artemis program, among its objectives, the flight will demonstrate life support systems for the first time with crew and lay the foundation for an enduring presence on the Moon ahead of future missions to Mars.

After reaching space, Orion deployed its solar array wings, enabling the spacecraft to receive energy from the Sun, while the crew and engineers on the ground immediately began transitioning the spacecraft from launch to flight operations to start checking out key systems.

“Artemis II is a test flight, and the test has just begun. The team that built this vehicle, repaired it, and prepared it for flight has given our crew the machine they need to go prove what it can do,” said NASA Associate Administrator Amit Kshatriya. “Over the next 10 days, Reid, Victor, Christina, and Jeremy will put Orion through its paces so the crews who follow them can go to the Moon’s surface with confidence. We are one mission into a long campaign, and the work ahead of us is greater than the work behind us.”

About 49 minutes into the test flight, the SLS rocket’s upper stage fired to put Orion into an elliptical orbit around Earth. A second planned burn by the stage will propel Orion, which the crew named “Integrity,” into a high Earth orbit extending about 46,000 miles beyond Earth. After the burn, Orion will separate from the stage, flying free on its own.

In several hours, a ring on the rocket’s upper stage, which will be a safe distance away from the spacecraft, will deploy four CubeSats – small satellites from Argentina’s Comisión Nacional de Actividades Espaciales, German Aerospace Center, Korea AeroSpace Administration, and Saudi Space Agency – to perform scientific investigations and technology demonstrations.

The spacecraft will remain in high Earth orbit for about a day, where the crew will conduct a manual pilot demonstration to test Orion’s handling capabilities. The astronauts, with Mission Control Center teams at NASA’s Johnson Space Center in Houston, will continue checking spacecraft systems.

If all systems remain healthy, mission controllers will give Orion’s European-built service module a command to conduct the translunar injection burn on Thursday, April 2. This move is an approximately six-minute firing to send the spacecraft on a trajectory that will simultaneously carry crew around the Moon, while also harnessing lunar gravity to slingshot them back to Earth.

During a planned multi-hour lunar flyby on Monday, April 6, the astronauts will take photographs and provide observations of the Moon’s surface as the first people to lay eyes on some areas of the far side. Although the lunar far side will only be partially illuminated during the flyby, the conditions should create shadows that stretch across the surface, enhancing relief and revealing depth, ridges, slopes and crater rims that are often difficult to detect under full illumination. Crew observations and other human health scientific investigations during the mission, such as AVATAR, will inform science during future Moon missions.

Following a successful lunar flyby, the astronauts will return to Earth and splash down in the Pacific Ocean.

As part of Golden Age of innovation and exploration, NASA will send Artemis astronauts on increasingly difficult missions to explore more of the Moon for scientific discovery, economic benefits, and to build on our foundation for the first crewed missions to Mars.

Follow the latest mission progress, including more images from the test flight, visit:

https://www.nasa.gov/mission/artemis-ii/

-end-

Bethany Stevens / Rachel Kraft

Headquarters, Washington

202-358-1100

bethany.c.stevens@nasa.gov / rachel.h.kraft@nasa.gov
articles.reasoning: The article reports on the successful launch of NASA's Artemis II mission, the first crewed lunar flyby in over 50 years, marking a significant milestone in human space exploration. This mission has broad significance as it lays the foundation for sustained human presence on the Moon and future Mars missions, with detailed context about the mission objectives, crew, and technology involved.
articles.personality_title: NASA launches Artemis II, first crewed lunar flyby in over 50 years
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA has been preparing to return humans to the Moon after more than 50 years since the last crewed mission. This new effort, called Artemis II, is part of a plan to explore the Moon more deeply and eventually send astronauts to Mars.

**What happened** – On April 1, 2026, NASA launched the Artemis II mission from Kennedy Space Center in Florida. A powerful rocket named the Space Launch System (SLS) carried four astronauts aboard the Orion spacecraft. The crew includes three NASA astronauts and one from the Canadian Space Agency. They are set to fly around the Moon and return to Earth in about 10 days.

**Impact** – This mission is important because it is the first time humans have flown around the Moon since the Apollo program in the 1970s. Artemis II will test life support systems with people on board and gather information to help future missions land on the Moon and stay longer. The mission also involves deploying small satellites to study space and testing Orion’s ability to be controlled by the astronauts.

**What's next step** – After orbiting Earth and testing the spacecraft, the crew will perform a special rocket burn to send them around the Moon. They will take pictures and make observations, including some of the Moon’s far side, which is rarely seen by humans. If all goes well, the astronauts will return safely to Earth, helping NASA prepare for more challenging missions in the coming years.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s Artemis II mission has successfully launched the first crewed lunar flyby in over five decades, marking a key step toward longer human stays on the Moon and future Mars exploration.
articles.personality_title_fr: La NASA lance Artemis II, le premier survol lunaire habité depuis plus de 50 ans
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La NASA se prépare à renvoyer des humains sur la Lune, plus de 50 ans après la dernière mission habitée. Cette nouvelle mission, appelée Artemis II, fait partie d’un plan pour explorer la Lune en profondeur et envoyer un jour des astronautes sur Mars.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 1er avril 2026, la NASA a lancé la mission Artemis II depuis le Kennedy Space Center en Floride. Une puissante fusée nommée Space Launch System (SLS) a transporté quatre astronautes à bord du vaisseau Orion. L’équipage comprend trois astronautes de la NASA et un de l’Agence spatiale canadienne. Ils vont faire le tour de la Lune et revenir sur Terre en environ 10 jours.

**Impact** – Cette mission est importante car c’est la première fois que des humains volent autour de la Lune depuis le programme Apollo dans les années 1970. Artemis II testera les systèmes de survie avec l’équipage à bord et collectera des données pour aider les futures missions à atterrir et rester plus longtemps sur la Lune. La mission comprend aussi le lancement de petits satellites pour étudier l’espace et tester la capacité de pilotage du vaisseau par les astronautes.

**Prochaine étape** – Après avoir orbité autour de la Terre et testé le vaisseau, l’équipage effectuera un tir de fusée spécial pour les envoyer autour de la Lune. Ils prendront des photos et feront des observations, y compris de la face cachée de la Lune, rarement vue par des humains. Si tout se passe bien, les astronautes reviendront sains et saufs sur Terre, aidant la NASA à préparer des missions plus difficiles dans les années à venir.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La mission Artemis II de la NASA a lancé avec succès le premier survol lunaire habité en plus de cinquante ans, marquant une étape clé vers des séjours humains plus longs sur la Lune et l’exploration future de Mars.
articles.personality_title_es: NASA lanza Artemis II, el primer sobrevuelo lunar tripulado en más de 50 años
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La NASA se está preparando para enviar humanos de nuevo a la Luna, más de 50 años después de la última misión tripulada. Esta nueva misión, llamada Artemis II, forma parte de un plan para explorar la Luna más a fondo y, en el futuro, llevar astronautas a Marte.

**Qué pasó** – El 1 de abril de 2026, la NASA lanzó la misión Artemis II desde el Centro Espacial Kennedy en Florida. Un cohete potente llamado Space Launch System (SLS) llevó a cuatro astronautas a bordo de la nave Orion. La tripulación incluye tres astronautas de la NASA y uno de la Agencia Espacial Canadiense. Volarán alrededor de la Luna y regresarán a la Tierra en unos 10 días.

**Impacto** – Esta misión es importante porque es la primera vez que humanos vuelan alrededor de la Luna desde el programa Apolo en los años 70. Artemis II probará sistemas de soporte vital con tripulación a bordo y reunirá información para ayudar a futuras misiones a aterrizar y quedarse más tiempo en la Luna. La misión también lanzará pequeños satélites para estudiar el espacio y probar la capacidad de control de Orion por parte de los astronautas.

**Próximo paso** – Después de orbitar la Tierra y probar la nave, la tripulación realizará una maniobra especial para enviarlos alrededor de la Luna. Tomarán fotos y harán observaciones, incluyendo la cara oculta de la Luna, que pocas veces han visto humanos. Si todo va bien, los astronautas regresarán sanos y salvos a la Tierra, ayudando a la NASA a preparar misiones más difíciles en el futuro.

**Frase clave** – La misión Artemis II de la NASA ha lanzado con éxito el primer sobrevuelo lunar tripulado en más de cinco décadas, marcando un paso clave hacia estancias humanas más largas en la Luna y la exploración futura de Marte.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a majestic rocket ascending from a coastal launch pad at sunset, its bright plume forming a glowing trail that curves toward a stylized crescent Moon in the sky, with four glowing stars arranged near the Moon symbolizing the Artemis II crew, all rendered in soft, natural earth tones and gentle blues.

articles.title: Evidence of the pair-instability gap from black-hole masses
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 01 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10359-0LIGO–Virgo–KAGRA’s fourth Gravitational-Wave Transient Catalog shows evidence of a clear pair-instability gap in the distribution of binary black-hole secondary masses but is absent in the larger primary masses.

articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery providing clear evidence of the pair-instability gap in black-hole masses from gravitational-wave data. This breakthrough enhances understanding of stellar evolution and black hole formation, impacting astrophysics broadly. The finding is based on extensive data from major gravitational-wave observatories and has wide implications for the scientific community and public knowledge about the universe.
articles.personality_title: New gravitational-wave data reveals a missing range in black hole sizes
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long predicted a gap in the sizes of black holes caused by a process called pair-instability in massive stars. This gap means black holes within a certain mass range should not form because the stars explode before collapsing into black holes.

**What happened** – Using data from the fourth catalog of gravitational waves detected by LIGO, Virgo, and KAGRA observatories, researchers found clear evidence of this predicted gap. They noticed that the smaller black holes in pairs show this missing range, confirming the pair-instability gap, while the larger black holes do not show it as clearly.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it confirms a key idea about how massive stars live and die. It helps scientists better understand black hole formation and the life cycles of stars. The finding also improves models of the universe and the way black holes grow and merge.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue analyzing new gravitational-wave signals to learn more about black hole populations. They aim to refine their understanding of the gap and explore how it affects the evolution of stars and black holes over time.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New gravitational-wave observations provide strong proof of a missing range in black hole masses, confirming predictions about how massive stars explode before becoming black holes.
articles.personality_title_fr: De nouvelles données d’ondes gravitationnelles révèlent une plage manquante dans les tailles des trous noirs
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques ont longtemps prédit une lacune dans les tailles des trous noirs causée par un processus appelé instabilité de paire dans les étoiles massives. Cette lacune signifie que les trous noirs d’une certaine masse ne devraient pas se former car les étoiles explosent avant de s’effondrer en trous noirs.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Grâce aux données du quatrième catalogue d’ondes gravitationnelles détectées par les observatoires LIGO, Virgo et KAGRA, les chercheurs ont trouvé une preuve claire de cette lacune prédite. Ils ont remarqué que les trous noirs plus petits dans les paires montrent cette plage manquante, confirmant la lacune d’instabilité de paire, alors que les trous noirs plus grands ne la montrent pas aussi clairement.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle confirme une idée clé sur la vie et la mort des étoiles massives. Elle aide les scientifiques à mieux comprendre la formation des trous noirs et le cycle de vie des étoiles. Cette preuve améliore aussi les modèles de l’univers et la façon dont les trous noirs grandissent et fusionnent.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs continueront à analyser de nouveaux signaux d’ondes gravitationnelles pour en apprendre plus sur les populations de trous noirs. Ils veulent affiner leur compréhension de cette lacune et explorer comment elle affecte l’évolution des étoiles et des trous noirs au fil du temps.

**Résumé en une phrase** – De nouvelles observations d’ondes gravitationnelles fournissent une preuve solide d’une plage manquante dans les masses des trous noirs, confirmant les prédictions sur la façon dont les étoiles massives explosent avant de devenir des trous noirs.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevos datos de ondas gravitacionales revelan un rango faltante en los tamaños de agujeros negros
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han predicho durante mucho tiempo un espacio vacío en los tamaños de los agujeros negros causado por un proceso llamado inestabilidad por pares en estrellas muy grandes. Este espacio significa que no deberían formarse agujeros negros dentro de cierto rango de masa porque las estrellas explotan antes de convertirse en agujeros negros.

**Qué pasó** – Usando datos del cuarto catálogo de ondas gravitacionales detectadas por los observatorios LIGO, Virgo y KAGRA, los investigadores encontraron una evidencia clara de este espacio vacío predicho. Notaron que los agujeros negros más pequeños en pares muestran esta ausencia, confirmando la brecha de inestabilidad por pares, mientras que los agujeros negros más grandes no la muestran tan claramente.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque confirma una idea clave sobre cómo viven y mueren las estrellas grandes. Ayuda a los científicos a entender mejor cómo se forman los agujeros negros y el ciclo de vida de las estrellas. El hallazgo también mejora los modelos del universo y cómo crecen y se fusionan los agujeros negros.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores continuarán analizando nuevas señales de ondas gravitacionales para aprender más sobre las poblaciones de agujeros negros. Quieren mejorar su comprensión de la brecha y explorar cómo afecta la evolución de las estrellas y los agujeros negros con el tiempo.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Nuevas observaciones de ondas gravitacionales ofrecen una fuerte prueba de un rango faltante en las masas de agujeros negros, confirmando predicciones sobre cómo las estrellas grandes explotan antes de convertirse en agujeros negros.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a cosmic landscape showing a stylized, glowing gap between two clusters of black holes—one cluster with smaller black holes and another with much larger ones—connected by subtle waves symbolizing gravitational waves, all set against a softly illuminated starry background with gentle nebula clouds in natural blues, purples, and warm oranges.

articles.title: 'We go for all humanity' - emotional moment as Artemis II blasts off
articles.summary: There was giddy euphoria at the Kennedy Space Center after the launch, writes the BBC's Pallab Ghosh.
articles.content: But the SLS launch was not only more beautiful, it meant much more: a moment full of emotion for all those who saw it, perhaps because it reminded us of what humanity can do when it comes together, or perhaps because we may be entering a new era of space travel.
articles.reasoning: The article describes the successful launch of Artemis II, a significant event symbolizing human achievement and the potential beginning of a new era in space exploration, which has broad inspirational impact for humanity.
articles.personality_title: Artemis II launch marks a new chapter in space exploration
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Artemis II is a NASA mission designed to send astronauts beyond the Moon. It follows Artemis I, which was an uncrewed test flight. This mission is part of a larger plan to explore space more deeply and eventually send humans to Mars.

**What happened** – On its launch day, Artemis II successfully blasted off from Kennedy Space Center. The launch was watched by many people, who felt a strong emotional connection to the event. The rocket’s powerful lift-off was not only a technical success but also a moment of celebration for the teams involved and viewers worldwide.

**Impact** – This launch is important because it shows what humans can achieve when working together. It also signals the start of a new phase in space travel, one that could lead to more human missions beyond the Moon. The excitement and hope generated by Artemis II inspire people to think about exploring space in new ways.

**What’s next step** – After this launch, Artemis II will continue its journey around the Moon and return safely to Earth. NASA plans to use the lessons learned from this mission to prepare for future trips, including Artemis III, which aims to land astronauts on the Moon again.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Artemis II’s successful launch opens the door to a new era of human space exploration beyond the Moon.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le lancement d’Artemis II ouvre un nouveau chapitre dans l’exploration spatiale
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Artemis II est une mission de la NASA destinée à envoyer des astronautes au-delà de la Lune. Elle suit Artemis I, qui était un vol d’essai sans équipage. Cette mission fait partie d’un plan plus large pour explorer l’espace plus en profondeur et éventuellement envoyer des humains sur Mars.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le jour du lancement, Artemis II a décollé avec succès depuis le Centre spatial Kennedy. Le lancement a été suivi par de nombreuses personnes, qui ont ressenti un fort lien émotionnel avec cet événement. Le puissant décollage de la fusée a été non seulement une réussite technique, mais aussi un moment de célébration pour les équipes impliquées et les spectateurs du monde entier.

**Impact** – Ce lancement est important car il montre ce que les humains peuvent accomplir en travaillant ensemble. Il marque aussi le début d’une nouvelle phase dans le voyage spatial, qui pourrait mener à plus de missions humaines au-delà de la Lune. L’enthousiasme et l’espoir suscités par Artemis II inspirent les gens à imaginer l’exploration spatiale autrement.

**Prochaine étape** – Après ce lancement, Artemis II poursuivra son voyage autour de la Lune avant de revenir en toute sécurité sur Terre. La NASA prévoit d’utiliser les enseignements de cette mission pour préparer les futures expéditions, y compris Artemis III, qui vise à faire atterrir des astronautes sur la Lune à nouveau.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le lancement réussi d’Artemis II ouvre la porte à une nouvelle ère d’exploration spatiale humaine au-delà de la Lune.
articles.personality_title_es: El lanzamiento de Artemis II marca un nuevo capítulo en la exploración espacial
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Artemis II es una misión de la NASA diseñada para enviar astronautas más allá de la Luna. Sigue a Artemis I, que fue un vuelo de prueba sin tripulación. Esta misión forma parte de un plan más amplio para explorar el espacio en mayor profundidad y eventualmente enviar humanos a Marte.

**Qué pasó** – El día del lanzamiento, Artemis II despegó con éxito desde el Centro Espacial Kennedy. Muchas personas vieron el lanzamiento y sintieron una fuerte conexión emocional con el evento. El poderoso despegue del cohete fue no solo un éxito técnico, sino también un momento de celebración para los equipos involucrados y espectadores en todo el mundo.

**Impacto** – Este lanzamiento es importante porque muestra lo que los humanos pueden lograr trabajando juntos. También señala el comienzo de una nueva etapa en los viajes espaciales, que podría llevar a más misiones humanas más allá de la Luna. La emoción y esperanza generadas por Artemis II inspiran a la gente a imaginar nuevas formas de explorar el espacio.

**Próximo paso** – Después de este lanzamiento, Artemis II continuará su viaje alrededor de la Luna y regresará a la Tierra de forma segura. La NASA planea usar las lecciones aprendidas en esta misión para preparar futuras misiones, incluyendo Artemis III, que tiene como objetivo llevar astronautas a la Luna nuevamente.

**Conclusión en una frase** – El exitoso lanzamiento de Artemis II abre la puerta a una nueva era de exploración espacial humana más allá de la Luna.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a majestic rocket ascending into a softly glowing sky, its fiery trail blending gently with stars and swirling cosmic colors, while abstract silhouettes of diverse hands reaching upward surround the launch, symbolizing unity and humanity’s collective hope for a new era of space exploration.
